fr:mainmatter.html:p: The main connective of a compound sentence is the last connective used in forming the sentence. Atomic sentences have no main connective.

Sentences can be categorised by their main connectives. So a conjunction is a sentence whose main connective is conjunction; a implication is a sentence whose main connective is implication; and so on for any connective.

Every compound sentence has a main connective. If you think of a sentence as being put together in stages, the main connective is the last connective used in assembling the sentence. If you write the sentence as a tree, the main connective is the one at the very top of the tree. No sentence can have more than one main connective: atomic sentences don't have any main connective at all (since they don't contain any connectives!), and every compound sentence has exactly one main connective.

So, for example, in the sentence p \land (q \lor \bot ), the main connective is conjunction, \land . At the last stage of its construction, this sentence uses \land to combine two other sentences: p and q \lor \bot . So while other connectives, \lor and \bot , do occur in the sentence, they're not its main connective. Its tree looks like this:

As you can see, this tree has the connective \land at the very top; that's another way to see that \land is the main connective of this sentence. (Note that nullary connectives like \bot go into trees in just the same way as atomic sentences: at the tips of branches.)

As another example, the main connective of \bot is \bot . This sentence is its own main connective, since that's the last connective used in building the sentence. Another one: the main connective of \neg (p \lor p) is \neg . Here's its tree:

It's often useful to categorize sentences by their main connective. So for example, we might want to think about all the sentences whose main connective is conjunction, \land . These sentences are called conjunctions; the category of sentences just takes its name from the connective name. Similarly, the sentences whose main connective is disjunction \lor are called disjunctions; the sentences whose main connective is negation \neg are called negations; and we will do the same kind of thing for every connective we come across. (There is only one sentence whose main connective is falsum: just \bot itself. The same happens for any nullary connective.) To sum this up:
The main connective of a compound sentence is the last connective used in forming the sentence. Atomic sentences have no main connective.

Sentences can be categorised by their main connectives. So a conjunction is a sentence whose main connective is conjunction; a implication is a sentence whose main connective is implication; and so on for any connective.

In general, we'll be working with sentences in different ways depending on their main connective. So it's important to be completely fluent in taking a sentence and identifying its main connective. To that end, the first exercise type you'll see at https://binchicken.one will give you practice with this: it will give you a random sentence, and your task is to identify the main connective of that sentence.
